statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
For an integer $ x $ , we call another integer $ y $ friendly if the following condition holds:

- $ y - d(y) = x $ , where $ d(y) $ is the sum of the digits of $ y $ .

For a given integer $ x $ , determine how many friendly numbers it has.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 500 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

Each test case consists of a single line containing one integer $ x $ ( $ 1 \le x \le 10^{9} $ ).

Output Format
For each test case, output one integer — the answer to the problem.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
1
18
998244360
Output
0
10
10

Note
The number $ 1 $ does not have any friendly numbers.

The number $ 18 $ has $ 10 $ friendly numbers: These are all the numbers from $ 20 $ to $ 29 $ . For example, $ 20 - d(20) = 20 - 2 = 18 $ .

The number $ 998\,244\,360 $ has $ 10 $ friendly numbers:

- $ 998\,244\,400 $
- $ 998\,244\,401 $
- $ 998\,244\,402 $
- $ 998\,244\,403 $
- $ 998\,244\,404 $
- $ 998\,244\,405 $
- $ 998\,244\,406 $
- $ 998\,244\,407 $
- $ 998\,244\,408 $
- $ 998\,244\,409 $

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2197A - Friendly Numbers
To solve this problem, it is sufficient to explicitly check that the number $$$i$$$ meets the condition for all $$$i$$$ from $$$x$$$ to $$$x + 83$$$. To verify this, we need to explicitly calculate $$$i - d(i)$$$. Now let's prove this.
Notice that numbers greater than $$$2 \cdot 10^9$$$ definitely do not suit us, since the sum of the digits of the number $$$2 \cdot 10^9 + y \cdot 10^9$$$ does not exceed $$$9 \cdot 9 + 2 + y = 83 + y$$$, while the value of $$$i$$$ in this case will be at least $$$(2 + y) \cdot 10^9$$$, which obviously cannot be suitable.
Thus, the maximum sum of the numbers we are interested in does not exceed $$$83$$$, which means that checking is relevant between $$$x$$$ and $$$x + 83$$$
